topic: A teacher receives an unexpected gift from a former student
raw_output:
[story 1]

Professor Eleanor Vance stared at the small, intricately carved wooden box resting on her desk. It was a delicate thing, inlaid with mother-of-pearl, and it was undeniably addressed to her. Her heart began to thump a frantic rhythm against her ribs. She traced the fine lines of the inscription with a trembling finger. It was from Marcus, a student she hadn't seen in five years, who had left university unexpectedly. Eleanor picked up the box, its weight feeling strangely heavy in her palm. Every instinct screamed at her to examine it closely, to search for a hidden meaning, a trap. She set it down, the sound echoing loudly in the quiet office, and avoided looking at the door, waiting for something to happen.

[story 2]

"Oh, my goodness," whispered Mr. Harrison, clutching the envelope as if it might dissolve. The package, addressed to him, contained a collection of rare, first-edition poetry books—a gift from Clara, a bright, spirited student from his sophomore year. Harrison’s hands were slick with perspiration as he carefully peeled back the wax seal. He looked around his cluttered study, suddenly hyper-aware of every shadow stretching across the walls. He reread the short, handwritten note tucked inside. A prickling sensation ran down his spine, making the fine hairs on his arms stand up. He needed to put the books down immediately; the silence of the room suddenly felt oppressive, charged with unspoken implications.

[story 3]

I picked up the small, silver locket. It was tucked beneath a stack of ungraded papers, a tangible artifact from Liam, a student who had graduated last spring. My breath hitched in my throat. The metal felt cold against my fingertips, and I could feel the rapid flutter beneath my sternum. I turned the locket over and over, trying to find a seam, a catch, anything to suggest it was a trick. I glanced toward the window, the afternoon light seeming too harsh, too exposing. I needed to put it somewhere safe, somewhere out of sight, before I could even begin to process what this unexpected item meant.
